html:
Margareta Eriksdotter may refer to:

  • Margaret of Denmark (in Sweden, Margareta Eriksdotter), a Danish princess and Swedish queen.
  • Margareta Eriksdotter (of the Eric family) (1155–1209), a Swedish princess and Norwegian queen.
  • Margareta Eriksdotter (of the Vasa family) (died in 1536), sister of Gustav Vasa.
  • Margareta Eriksdotter (of Leijonhufvud) (1516–1551), the second wife of Gustav Vasa, and Queen of Sweden from 1536 to 1551.
  • Margareta Eriksdotter (of Krummedige) (1390-1451).

How certain are we about this?

From the Vasa era onwards, individuals are well documented in archives. The uncertainty lies more in interpretation and how later generations perceived them – not least because those in power themselves played a role in shaping historical narratives.
tldr: The text explains that the name "Margareta Eriksdotter" could refer to several different historical women, including queens and princesses.
lattlast:
The name Margareta Eriksdotter is common in historical texts, but it can refer to several different individuals. It could be a Danish princess who became Queen of Sweden, or other women from royal families during the Middle Ages.

Because this name was used by many important women throughout history, it's crucial to know which person is being discussed. The text provides several examples of these women, including sisters and wives of kings.

From the 16th century onwards, a great deal of information about how these individuals lived has been preserved in archives. What can be difficult to ascertain with certainty is how they were perceived by other people in the past, as power often dictated how history was recorded.
